source.sourcePolicy: Rules and flow are compiled only from the jurisdiction reference/source documents supplied in the Nationwide archive, plus the user-supplied Nebraska reference.

pathwayLabels: Misdemeanors Where Expungement Is Not Presumed

questions.prompt: Are you asking about your own record?
options: I am helping someone complete their own check

questions.prompt: Is this a state or local matter from Montana, rather than a federal matter?
How did the Montana case or record end?
options: Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted
Diversion, deferred disposition, supervision, or similar program

questions.prompt: What level or type was the charge?
Do any of these Montana descriptions sound close to your situation?
options: None of these / I am not sure
questions.helper: This answer is optional and does not select your legal pathway. The backend uses the factual answers and state rules.

questions.prompt: 1 Was this case in Montana state court, Tribal court, federal court, or another state?
2 Was the case a conviction, dismissal, acquittal, dropped charge, not-filed arrest, deferred prosecution, or deferred sentence?
3 Was the offense a misdemeanor, felony, or marijuana-related offense?
4 Which court handled it: District Court, Justice Court, City Court, Municipal Court, or another court?
5 What county?
6 What was the charge and statute number?
7 What was the conviction date?
8 When were all sentence terms completed?
9 Were all fines, fees, restitution, jail time, treatment, and probation completed?
10 Has the user had any new convictions in Montana, another state, or federal court in the five years after completing the sentence?
11 Does the user have any pending charges, open cases, or current detention?
12 Has the user ever successfully petitioned for Montana misdemeanor expungement before?
13 Was the offense assault, partner/family member assault, stalking, sexual assault, protective-order violation, DUI, or DUI-enhanced?
14 Was there a victim?
15 Was the case marijuana-related?
16 Was the person currently serving the sentence or has it been completed?
17 Was the sentence deferred and, if so, has dismissal already been entered?
18 Does the user need expungement for employment, housing, licensing, school, immigration, military enlistment, or firearms?
Check five-year completion rule, no pending charges, no new conviction, sentence completion, and prior expungement history.
How old were you when the offense or case began?
Have you completed every part of the sentence, supervision, probation, parole, classes, or community service?
Are all fines, restitution, court costs, and other required financial obligations resolved?
Do you currently have any pending criminal charge, open case, probation, parole, or other supervision?
Have you previously received record-clearing relief in Montana or elsewhere?
Have you received a pardon, certificate of rehabilitation, or similar executive relief for this conviction?
Do you have the Montana criminal-history report, court docket, or certified disposition needed to verify the record?
When was the case disposed of, dismissed, completed, or discharged?
What Montana county, parish, borough, district, or local filing area handled the case?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What charge, statute, or record description appears on the record?
Do you have court paperwork or a criminal-history record showing the exact charge and outcome?

caseOutcomeOptions.label: Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted
Diversion, deferred disposition, supervision, or similar program

pathways.label: Misdemeanor-conviction expungement under Mont. Code § 46-18-1104
pathways.summary: A person should generally use the Montana misdemeanor expungement packet only if all of these are true: Montana misdemeanor conviction Must be one or more misdemeanors in Montana state court. Sentence complete Court fines, jail time, treatment, and probation must be completed. Five-year clean period Five years must have passed since completion of sentence terms. No pending/open criminal case Person must not be jailed, charged, or have pending/open cases for a new offense.
ruleClauses: A person should generally use the Montana misdemeanor expungement packet only if all of these are true:
Montana misdemeanor conviction Must be one or more misdemeanors in Montana state court.
Sentence complete Court fines, jail time, treatment, and probation must be completed.
Five-year clean period Five years must have passed since completion of sentence terms.
No pending/open criminal case Person must not be jailed, charged, or have pending/open cases for a new offense.
No prior successful expungement Montana misdemeanor expungement is once in a lifetime.
These requirements are stated in the official 2025 self-represented misdemeanor expungement packet and in Montanas misdemeanor expungement statute.
Expungement is presumed if the person is not currently detained, charged, or facing pending charges for a new offense, and either:
1 the person has had no convictions in Montana, another state, or federal court for five years after completing the sentence terms, including financial obligations and court-ordered treatment; or

2 the person has applied to a U.S. military academy, applied to enlist in the armed forces or National Guard, or is currently serving and is being held back because of the conviction. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0070/0460-0180-0110-0070.html")
Wilma should calculate the waiting period from:
the date all sentencing terms were completed,

Montana allows a person to petition under the misdemeanor expungement statute no more than one time during the persons life. Because of that, the court packet tells users it is best to list every misdemeanor conviction they want expunged. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0040/0460-0180-0110-0040.html")
Montana misdemeanor expungement is generally a one-time opportunity, so you should identify every misdemeanor conviction you may want included before filing.
Main misdemeanor law MCA Title 46, Chapter 18, Part 11
Main misdemeanor petition statute 46-18-1104
Venue District Court where at least one misdemeanor conviction occurred
Waiting period Generally 5 years after completing sentence terms
Pending charges No current detention, charges, or pending new offense
Fingerprints Required for identity validation
Not-presumed offenses Assault, PFMA, stalking, sexual assault, protective order, DUI, DUI-enhanced
Court standard Preponderance of the evidence
Prosecutor notice Serve every prosecution office involved
Victim notice Prosecutor attempts notice within 14 days
Court can grant All, some, or none of the requested misdemeanors
DOJ follow-up Send order, fingerprint card, and DOJ form to CRISS
Non-convictions CRISS removal/sealing under 44-5-202 process
Deferred sentence Dismissal can make records confidential
waitingRules: Five-year clean period Five years must have passed since completion of sentence terms.
1 the person has had no convictions in Montana, another state, or federal court for five years after completing the sentence terms, including financial obligations and court-ordered treatment; or
Wilma should calculate the waiting period from:

If the court grants expungement, the petitioner must complete the Montana DOJ follow-up process. DOJ says the petitioner must submit the court order, expungement form, and fingerprint card to CRISS, and CRISS verifies the person has not previously used the law and confirms identity before removing arrest/conviction data from the Computerized Criminal History system. DOJ says to allow about 30 days for processing. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/conviction-expungement-process/")
[Five years met / not met / not applicable / unknown]
Waiting period Generally 5 years after completing sentence terms
Victim notice Prosecutor attempts notice within 14 days

Topic Montana Rule Main misdemeanor law MCA Title 46, Chapter 18, Part 11 Main misdemeanor petition statute 46-18-1104 Venue District Court where at least one misdemeanor conviction occurred Waiting period Generally 5 years after completing sentence terms Pending charges No current detention, charges, or pending new offense Once in lifetime Yes Fingerprints Required for identity validation Not-presumed offenses Assault, PFMA, stalking, sexual assault, protective order, DUI, DUI-enhanced Court standard Preponderance of the evidence Prosecutor notice Serve every prosecution office involved Victim notice Prosecutor attempts notice within 14 days Court can grant All, some, or none of the requested misdemeanors DOJ follow-up Send order, fingerprint card, and DOJ form to CRISS Non-convictions CRISS removal/sealing under 44-5-202 process Deferred sentence Dismissal can make records confidential Marijuana Separate MMRTA forms and process Felony, non-marijuana Generally not covered by misdemeanor expungement Tribal/out-of-state/federal Not covered by Montana misdemeanor packet This Montana reference is ready for Wilma training, intake triage, eligibility screening, document routing, and escalation logic.

pathways.label: Non-conviction criminal-history removal through CRISS
pathways.summary: If Tribal, federal, or out-of-state Montanas misdemeanor expungement packet does not cover this case. This needs jurisdiction-specific review. Possible Montana DOJ CRISS non-conviction removal or sealing. Check whether the charge was dismissed, acquitted, dropped, not filed, or dismissed after deferred prosecution. If deferred sentence not dismissed Contact the sentencing court about dismissal after deferred imposition before record-sealing analysis.

Contact the sentencing court about dismissal after deferred imposition before record-sealing analysis.
If deferred sentence already dismissed
Records may already be confidential criminal justice information. Check whether the public record still appears and whether DOJ CRISS needs documentation.
Use Montana MMRTA pathway: Form A if currently serving sentence, Form B if completed sentence. File in the court of conviction and serve county attorney.
Check five-year completion rule, no pending charges, no new conviction, sentence completion, and prior expungement history.
If misdemeanor is in not-presumed category
Expungement is not presumed. Collect rehabilitation evidence and recommend attorney or legal clinic review.
No general Montana felony expungement path through the misdemeanor packet. Check for deferred-sentence dismissal, marijuana-specific relief, pardon, or attorney review.

Any missing court documentation needed by CRISS.

DOJ says to allow about 30 days for processing. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/conviction-expungement-process/")

pathways.label: Deferred-sentence dismissal or confidentiality route
pathways.summary: [No prior use / prior use / unknown] Risk Flags: [Felony, DUI, assault, PFMA, stalking, sexual assault, protective order, victim, immigration, firearms, licensing, Tribal, federal, out-of-state] Next Step: [Get CRISS record, get final judgment, complete misdemeanor packet, complete MMRTA Form A/B, request deferred dismissal, or consult attorney] Disclaimer: When granted, the order must direct the arresting law-enforcement agency, prosecutors office, and clerk of court to permanently seal records of arrest, investigation, detention, and court proceedings. The Montana DOJ must expunge state records of arrest, investigation, detention, and…
pathways.sourceRef: section:389-399; section:185-203; section:204-228; section:229-269; section:400-421; section:270-318; section:362-367; summary:400-421
ruleClauses: [No prior use / prior use / unknown]
Risk Flags: [Felony, DUI, assault, PFMA, stalking, sexual assault, protective order, victim, immigration, firearms, licensing, Tribal, federal, out-of-state]
Next Step: [Get CRISS record, get final judgment, complete misdemeanor packet, complete MMRTA Form A/B, request deferred dismissal, or consult attorney]
Disclaimer:

When granted, the order must direct the arresting law-enforcement agency, prosecutors office, and clerk of court to permanently seal records of arrest, investigation, detention, and court proceedings. The Montana DOJ must expunge state records of arrest, investigation, detention, and court proceedings addressed by the order after receiving the order, fingerprints, and DOJ form. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0100/0460-0180-0110-0100.html")

For non-convictions, DOJ says sealed records are not publicly accessible unless a District Court orders access upon good cause shown, but certain criminal justice agencies still have access. For removed non-convictions, DOJ describes the record as deleted from the database and hard-copy information destroyed. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/non-conviction-removal-and-sealing/")

After dismissal of a deferred sentence, records and data relating to the charge are confidential criminal justice information, and public access requires a District Court order upon good cause shown. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0020/section_0040/0460-0180-0020-0040.html")
Wilma should not promise that Montana relief will:
Clear Tribal court convictions.
Clear out-of-state convictions.
Remove private background-check copies automatically.
Remove every fingerprint or investigative record.

Montanas misdemeanor packet expressly excludes out-of-state and Tribal court convictions, and the statutory definition of record excludes fingerprint records and investigative data that may be maintained for investigative purposes.

I can help you understand possible Montana record-clearing pathways and gather the information needed for the right next step. This is legal information, not legal advice. Montana has different processes for misdemeanor convictions, non-convictions, deferred sentences, and marijuana-related convictions.
1 Was this case in Montana state court, Tribal court, federal court, or another state?
2 Was the case a conviction, dismissal, acquittal, dropped charge, not-filed arrest, deferred prosecution, or deferred sentence?
3 Was the offense a misdemeanor, felony, or marijuana-related offense?
4 Which court handled it: District Court, Justice Court, City Court, Municipal Court, or another court?
6 What was the charge and statute number?
7 What was the conviction date?
8 When were all sentence terms completed?
9 Were all fines, fees, restitution, jail time, treatment, and probation completed?
10 Has the user had any new convictions in Montana, another state, or federal court in the five years after completing the sentence?
11 Does the user have any pending charges, open cases, or current detention?
12 Has the user ever successfully petitioned for Montana misdemeanor expungement before?
13 Was the offense assault, partner/family member assault, stalking, sexual assault, protective-order violation, DUI, or DUI-enhanced?
15 Was the case marijuana-related?
16 Was the person currently serving the sentence or has it been completed?
waitingRules: Five-year clean period Five years must have passed since completion of sentence terms.
1 the person has had no convictions in Montana, another state, or federal court for five years after completing the sentence terms, including financial obligations and court-ordered treatment; or

If the court grants expungement, the petitioner must complete the Montana DOJ follow-up process. DOJ says the petitioner must submit the court order, expungement form, and fingerprint card to CRISS, and CRISS verifies the person has not previously used the law and confirms identity before removing arrest/conviction data from the Computerized Criminal History system. DOJ says to allow about 30 days for processing. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/conviction-expungement-process/")
[Five years met / not met / not applicable / unknown]
Waiting period Generally 5 years after completing sentence terms

pathways.label: Marijuana-related redesignation / expungement under MMRTA
pathways.summary: For marijuana-related cases, the Montana Judicial Branch provides: MMRTA Form A Currently serving sentence; request expungement or resentencing MMRTA Form B Sentence completed; request expungement or redesignation Proposed Order Submitted with petition Certificate of Service Shows service on county attorney
pathways.sourceRef: section:124-134; section:170-184; section:389-399; section:229-269; section:270-318; section:319-361; section:204-228; section:362-367
ruleClauses: For marijuana-related cases, the Montana Judicial Branch provides:
MMRTA Form A Currently serving sentence; request expungement or resentencing
MMRTA Form B Sentence completed; request expungement or redesignation
Proposed Order Submitted with petition
Certificate of Service Shows service on county attorney
The Montana Judicial Branch says Form A is for people currently serving a sentence, Form B is for people who completed the sentence, and each conviction needs a separate form. (
"https://courts.mt.gov/Forms/mmrta")
Step 1 - Confirm marijuana-related conviction
Wilma should ask whether the conviction involved marijuana/cannabis and identify the statute, charge, court, conviction date, and sentence status.
Step 2 - Determine whether sentence is current or completed
Form A if currently serving the sentence and seeking expungement or resentencing.
Form B if sentence is completed and seeking expungement or redesignation.

The Judicial Branch says the person must know the date or approximate date of conviction, the Montana court where it occurred, and must attach a copy of the criminal record from Montana DOJ. ("https://courts.mt.gov/Forms/mmrta")
Step 3 - File separate form for each conviction
Montana Judicial Branch says a separate form must be completed for each conviction, even if the convictions happened at the same time. ("https://courts.mt.gov/Forms/mmrta")

The petition must be served on the county attorney for the county where the petition is filed. Under the Judicial Branch instructions, the county attorney has 21 days to oppose the petition, and the court then considers the petition or sets a hearing if one was requested. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0160/chapter_0120/part_0010/section_0130/0160-0120-0010-0130.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

If the court grants marijuana-related expungement, the petitioner must file the DOJ Expungement/Removal Request Form with the Montana Department of Justice along with the court order. ("https://courts.mt.gov/Forms/mmrta")
[No prior use / prior use / unknown]
Risk Flags: [Felony, DUI, assault, PFMA, stalking, sexual assault, protective order, victim, immigration, firearms, licensing, Tribal, federal, out-of-state]
Next Step: [Get CRISS record, get final judgment, complete misdemeanor packet, complete MMRTA Form A/B, request deferred dismissal, or consult attorney]
Disclaimer:
If Tribal, federal, or out-of-state
Montanas misdemeanor expungement packet does not cover this case. This needs jurisdiction-specific review.
Possible Montana DOJ CRISS non-conviction removal or sealing. Check whether the charge was dismissed, acquitted, dropped, not filed, or dismissed after deferred prosecution.
If deferred sentence not dismissed
Contact the sentencing court about dismissal after deferred imposition before record-sealing analysis.
If deferred sentence already dismissed
Records may already be confidential criminal justice information. Check whether the public record still appears and whether DOJ CRISS needs documentation.
waitingRules: 1 the person has had no convictions in Montana, another state, or federal court for five years after completing the sentence terms, including financial obligations and court-ordered treatment; or

If the court grants expungement, the petitioner must complete the Montana DOJ follow-up process. DOJ says the petitioner must submit the court order, expungement form, and fingerprint card to CRISS, and CRISS verifies the person has not previously used the law and confirms identity before removing arrest/conviction data from the Computerized Criminal History system. DOJ says to allow about 30 days for processing. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/conviction-expungement-process/")

when.sourceConditionText: In Montana, expungement can remove qualifying misdemeanor conviction information from the state criminal history system and seal related court, prosecutor, and law-enforcement records. It does not mean every possible copy, fingerprint, federal record, Tribal record, or private-background-check copy disappears automatically.
Check five-year completion rule, no pending charges, no new conviction, sentence completion, and prior expungement history.
Waiting period Generally 5 years after completing sentence terms
Pending charges No current detention, charges, or pending new offense
Private background check companies will automatically update.
For Montanas misdemeanor expungement statute,
Montanas misdemeanor expungement packet does not cover this case. This needs jurisdiction-specific review.
Possible Montana DOJ CRISS non-conviction removal or sealing. Check whether the charge was dismissed, acquitted, dropped, not filed, or dismissed after deferred prosecution.
If deferred sentence not dismissed
Contact the sentencing court about dismissal after deferred imposition before record-sealing analysis.
If deferred sentence already dismissed
Records may already be confidential criminal justice information. Check whether the public record still appears and whether DOJ CRISS needs documentation.
Use Montana MMRTA pathway: Form A if currently serving sentence, Form B if completed sentence. File in the court of conviction and serve county attorney.
Expungement is not presumed. Collect rehabilitation evidence and recommend attorney or legal clinic review.
No general Montana felony expungement path through the misdemeanor packet. Check for deferred-sentence dismissal, marijuana-specific relief, pardon, or attorney review.

County and court for each conviction.

Conviction date or approximate date.
Whether sentence is current or completed.
Whether requesting expungement, resentencing, or redesignation.
DOJ Expungement/Removal Request after order.
Court disposition showing dismissal, acquittal, dropped charge, not filed, deferred prosecution dismissed, or invalidated conviction.
CRISS non-conviction removal request.
Wilma should recommend attorney or legal-clinic review if any of these apply:
Marijuana felony or resentencing/redesignation issue.
Tribal court, federal court, or out-of-state conviction.
New conviction during the five-year period.
Prior use of Montana misdemeanor expungement.
A felony can be expunged in Montana
without checking marijuana or deferred-sentence context.
You can file again later if you forget a misdemeanor.

Based on what you shared, you may have a Montana record-clearing pathway. The next step is to verify the official court record, Montana DOJ criminal history, sentence completion date, pending-case status, and whether the case is a misdemeanor, non-conviction, deferred sentence, or marijuana-related conviction.
Main misdemeanor petition statute 46-18-1104
Venue District Court where at least one misdemeanor conviction occurred
Non-convictions CRISS removal/sealing under 44-5-202 process
Deferred sentence Dismissal can make records confidential
Marijuana Separate MMRTA forms and process
Felony, non-marijuana Generally not covered by misdemeanor expungement
This Montana reference is ready for Wilma training, intake triage, eligibility screening, document routing, and escalation logic.
Misdemeanor-conviction expungement under Mont. Code § 46-18-1104
Non-conviction criminal-history removal through CRISS

waitingPeriodRules.ruleText: Wilma should calculate the waiting period from:

If the court grants expungement, the petitioner must complete the Montana DOJ follow-up process. DOJ says the petitioner must submit the court order, expungement form, and fingerprint card to CRISS, and CRISS verifies the person has not previously used the law and confirms identity before removing arrest/conviction data from the Computerized Criminal History system. DOJ says to allow about 30 days for processing. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/conviction-expungement-process/")
[Five years met / not met / not applicable / unknown]
Waiting period Generally 5 years after completing sentence terms
Victim notice Prosecutor attempts notice within 14 days

The person must send a copy of the order to the arresting agency, prosecutor, clerk of court, and Montana DOJ, along with fingerprints and the DOJ identifying-information form. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0100/0460-0180-0110-0100.html")

When granted, the order must direct the arresting law-enforcement agency, prosecutors office, and clerk of court to permanently seal records of arrest, investigation, detention, and court proceedings. The Montana DOJ must expunge state records of arrest, investigation, detention, and court proceedings addressed by the order after receiving the order, fingerprints, and DOJ form. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0100/0460-0180-0110-0100.html")

For non-convictions, DOJ says sealed records are not publicly accessible unless a District Court orders access upon good cause shown, but certain criminal justice agencies still have access. For removed non-convictions, DOJ describes the record as deleted from the database and hard-copy information destroyed. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/non-conviction-removal-and-sealing/")

After dismissal of a deferred sentence, records and data relating to the charge are confidential criminal justice information, and public access requires a District Court order upon good cause shown. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0020/section_0040/0460-0180-0020-0040.html")
Remove private background-check copies automatically.

Misdemeanor expungement effect When granted, the order must direct the arresting law-enforcement agency, prosecutors office, and clerk of court to permanently seal records of arrest, investigation, detention, and court proceedings.

The Montana DOJ must expunge state records of arrest, investigation, detention, and court proceedings addressed by the order after receiving the order, fingerprints, and DOJ form. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0100/0460-0180-0110-0100.html") Non-conviction effect For non-convictions, DOJ says sealed records are not publicly accessible unless a District Court orders access upon good cause shown, but certain criminal justice agencies still have access.

For removed non-convictions, DOJ describes the record as deleted from the database and hard-copy information destroyed. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/non-conviction-removal-and-sealing/") Deferred dismissal effect After dismissal of a deferred sentence, records and data relating to the charge are confidential criminal justice information, and public access requires a District Court order upon good cause shown. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0020/section_0040/0460-0180-0020-0040.html") Limits Wilma should not promise that Montana relief will: Remove federal records.
Not-presumed offenses Assault, PFMA, stalking, sexual assault, protective order, DUI, DUI-enhanced
DOJ follow-up Send order, fingerprint card, and DOJ form to CRISS
Non-convictions CRISS removal/sealing under 44-5-202 process
Felony, non-marijuana Generally not covered by misdemeanor expungement

copyGuardrails: Montanas definition of record includes arrest/detention descriptions, charging documents, dispositions, sentences, correctional status, release, and court documents, but it does not include fingerprint records or data maintained for investigative purposes. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0030/0460-0180-0110-0030.html") Wilma should explain it this way: In Montana, expungement can remove qualifying misdemeanor conviction information from the state criminal history system and seal related court, prosecutor, and law-enforcement records.
It does not mean every possible copy, fingerprint, federal record, Tribal record, or private-background-check copy disappears automatically.
Use Montana-specific remedy terminology returned by the profile; do not relabel every remedy as expungement.
Use “may qualify,” “possible path,” and “the court or agency makes the final decision.”
Do not promise erasure, sealing, eligibility, timing, filing acceptance, or a court outcome.
Do not show checkout unless the backend returns a packet-ready result and paymentAllowed=true.

sourceSections.title: Core Montana Rule
sourceSections.text:
For Montanas misdemeanor expungement statute,
expunge
means to permanently destroy, delete, or erase a record from the Montana Department of Justice criminal history record information system. Montanas definition of
record
includes arrest/detention descriptions, charging documents, dispositions, sentences, correctional status, release, and court documents, but it does not include fingerprint records or data maintained for investigative purposes. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0030/0460-0180-0110-0030.html")
Wilma should explain it this way:

In Montana, expungement can remove qualifying misdemeanor conviction information from the state criminal history system and seal related court, prosecutor, and law-enforcement records. It does not mean every possible copy, fingerprint, federal record, Tribal record, or private-background-check copy disappears automatically.

sourceSections.title: Misdemeanor Conviction Eligibility
sourceSections.text:
A person should generally use the Montana misdemeanor expungement packet only if all of these are true:

Requirement Wilma Rule
Montana misdemeanor conviction Must be one or more misdemeanors in Montana state court.
Sentence complete Court fines, jail time, treatment, and probation must be completed.
Five-year clean period Five years must have passed since completion of sentence terms.
No pending/open criminal case Person must not be jailed, charged, or have pending/open cases for a new offense.
No prior successful expungement Montana misdemeanor expungement is once in a lifetime.
These requirements are stated in the official 2025 self-represented misdemeanor expungement packet and in Montanas misdemeanor expungement statute.
Five-year rule
Expungement is presumed if the person is not currently detained, charged, or facing pending charges for a new offense, and either:

1 the person has had no convictions in Montana, another state, or federal court for five years after completing the sentence terms, including financial obligations and court-ordered treatment; or
2 the person has applied to a U.S. military academy, applied to enlist in the armed forces or National Guard, or is currently serving and is being held back because of the conviction. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0070/0460-0180-0110-0070.html")
Wilma should calculate the waiting period from:

the date all sentencing terms were completed,

not just the conviction date.
Once-in-a-lifetime rule
Montana allows a person to petition under the misdemeanor expungement statute no more than one time during the persons life. Because of that, the court packet tells users it is best to list every misdemeanor conviction they want expunged. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0040/0460-0180-0110-0040.html")
Wilma should warn:

Montana misdemeanor expungement is generally a one-time opportunity, so you should identify every misdemeanor conviction you may want included before filing.

sourceSections.title: Misdemeanors Where Expungement Is Not Presumed
sourceSections.text:
Some offenses are not automatically disqualified, but expungement is not presumed. Montana law says expungement may not be presumed if the person has one or more convictions for:

Not-Presumed Category Statutory Reference
Assault 45-5-201
Partner or family member assault 45-5-206
Stalking 45-5-220
Sexual assault 45-5-502
Violation of protective order 45-5-626
DUI under Title 61, chapter 8, part 10 DUI statutes
Any offense with enhanced penalty because of DUI DUI-enhanced offense
For these cases, the court considers the petitioners age at the time of the offense, time since the offense, rehabilitation, likelihood of reoffending, and any other relevant factor. (

"https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0080/0460-0180-0110-0080.html")
Wilma should not say these are impossible. Wilma should say:

This offense falls into a category where expungement is not presumed. The court may still consider it, but you should expect a more careful review and should consider legal help.

sourceSections.title: Misdemeanor Expungement Packet
sourceSections.text:
The official Montana self-represented misdemeanor expungement packet contains:

Form / Item Purpose
Instructions Step-by-step filing guidance
Petition for Expungement of Misdemeanor Records Main request to the District Court
Order Expunging Misdemeanor Records and Sealing Record of this Proceeding Proposed order for judge
Statement of Inability to Pay Court Costs and Fees Fee waiver request
Order Regarding Statement of Inability to Pay Court Costs Proposed fee-waiver order
The packet itself lists these documents and says it is for expunging past Montana misdemeanor convictions.

sourceSections.title: Marijuana-Related Forms
sourceSections.text:
For marijuana-related cases, the Montana Judicial Branch provides:
Form Use
MMRTA Form A Currently serving sentence; request expungement or resentencing
MMRTA Form B Sentence completed; request expungement or redesignation
Proposed Order Submitted with petition
Certificate of Service Shows service on county attorney
Instructions Explains process
The Montana Judicial Branch says Form A is for people currently serving a sentence, Form B is for people who completed the sentence, and each conviction needs a separate form. (

"https://courts.mt.gov/Forms/mmrta")

sourceSections.title: DOJ Expungement / Removal Request
sourceSections.text: If the court grants expungement, the petitioner must complete the Montana DOJ follow-up process. DOJ says the petitioner must submit the court order, expungement form, and fingerprint card to CRISS, and CRISS verifies the person has not previously used the law and confirms identity before removing arrest/conviction data from the Computerized Criminal History system. DOJ says to allow about 30 days for processing. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/conviction-expungement-process/")

sourceSections.title: Filing Process - Misdemeanor Convictions
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Identify every conviction
Wilma should guide the user to get both:
1 Montana DOJ CRISS criminal history record; and
2 Montana court records from the public access portals or the specific court.
The court packet warns that some misdemeanor convictions may not appear on the DOJ CRISS background check, so the user should also search Montanas public access portals for District Courts and Courts of Limited Jurisdiction.
Step 2 - Get final judgments
The court packet instructs users to obtain final judgments from the original courts, because the judgment is the document that records final convictions and may be needed after a successful expungement order.
Step 3 - Fill out petition
Wilma should collect:
Full legal name.
Mailing address.
Phone and email.
All misdemeanor convictions to include.
Court name for each conviction.
Approximate date of conviction.
Case number, if known.
Sentence completion date.
Confirmation that fines, restitution, jail, treatment, probation, and other sentence terms are complete.
Any pending charges or open cases.
Any new convictions within five years after completing sentence terms.
Prior expungement history.
Whether any conviction is in a not-presumed category.
Step 4 - Complete certificate of service
The packet instructs users to list all county attorney and city attorney offices that prosecuted the cases; if more than two offices must be notified, the user should add another page.
Step 5 - File in District Court
The petition is filed in a District Court where at least one misdemeanor conviction occurred. The user should file the petition, proposed order, certificate of service, and fee waiver documents if needed. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0050/0460-0180-0110-0050.html")
Step 6 - Court review / hearing
The court may hold a hearing, especially if the prosecutor or victim responds. The court can grant expungement for all, some, or none of the misdemeanor offenses requested. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0090/0460-0180-0110-0090.html")
Step 7 - Send order to agencies
If expungement is granted, Montana law requires the order to direct the arresting law-enforcement agency, prosecutors office, and clerk of court to permanently seal records. The person must send a copy of the order to the arresting agency, prosecutor, clerk of court, and Montana DOJ, along with fingerprints and the DOJ identifying-information form. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0100/0460-0180-0110-0100.html")

sourceSections.title: Filing Process - Marijuana-Related Convictions
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Confirm marijuana-related conviction
Wilma should ask whether the conviction involved marijuana/cannabis and identify the statute, charge, court, conviction date, and sentence status.
Step 2 - Determine whether sentence is current or completed
Use:
Form A if currently serving the sentence and seeking expungement or resentencing.
Form B if sentence is completed and seeking expungement or redesignation.
The Judicial Branch says the person must know the date or approximate date of conviction, the Montana court where it occurred, and must attach a copy of the criminal record from Montana DOJ. ("https://courts.mt.gov/Forms/mmrta")
Step 3 - File separate form for each conviction
Montana Judicial Branch says a separate form must be completed for each conviction, even if the convictions happened at the same time. ("https://courts.mt.gov/Forms/mmrta")
Step 4 - Serve county attorney
The petition must be served on the county attorney for the county where the petition is filed. Under the Judicial Branch instructions, the county attorney has 21 days to oppose the petition, and the court then considers the petition or sets a hearing if one was requested. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0160/chapter_0120/part_0010/section_0130/0160-0120-0010-0130.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Step 5 - DOJ follow-up
If the court grants marijuana-related expungement, the petitioner must file the DOJ Expungement/Removal Request Form with the Montana Department of Justice along with the court order. ("https://courts.mt.gov/Forms/mmrta")

sourceSections.title: Effects and Limits of Montana Relief
sourceSections.text:
Misdemeanor expungement effect
When granted, the order must direct the arresting law-enforcement agency, prosecutors office, and clerk of court to permanently seal records of arrest, investigation, detention, and court proceedings. The Montana DOJ must expunge state records of arrest, investigation, detention, and court proceedings addressed by the order after receiving the order, fingerprints, and DOJ form. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0110/section_0100/0460-0180-0110-0100.html")
Non-conviction effect
For non-convictions, DOJ says sealed records are not publicly accessible unless a District Court orders access upon good cause shown, but certain criminal justice agencies still have access. For removed non-convictions, DOJ describes the record as deleted from the database and hard-copy information destroyed. ("https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/non-conviction-removal-and-sealing/")
Deferred dismissal effect
After dismissal of a deferred sentence, records and data relating to the charge are confidential criminal justice information, and public access requires a District Court order upon good cause shown. ("https://mca.legmt.gov/bills/mca/title_0460/chapter_0180/part_0020/section_0040/0460-0180-0020-0040.html")
Limits
Wilma should not promise that Montana relief will:
Remove federal records.
Remove FBI records.
Clear Tribal court convictions.
Clear out-of-state convictions.
Remove private background-check copies automatically.
Restore firearms rights.
Fix immigration consequences.
Remove every fingerprint or investigative record.
Montanas misdemeanor packet expressly excludes out-of-state and Tribal court convictions, and the statutory definition of record excludes fingerprint records and investigative data that may be maintained for investigative purposes.

sourceSections.title: Wilma Intake Script
sourceSections.text:
Opening

I can help you understand possible Montana record-clearing pathways and gather the information needed for the right next step. This is legal information, not legal advice. Montana has different processes for misdemeanor convictions, non-convictions, deferred sentences, and marijuana-related convictions.

Core Questions
1 Was this case in Montana state court, Tribal court, federal court, or another state?
2 Was the case a conviction, dismissal, acquittal, dropped charge, not-filed arrest, deferred prosecution, or deferred sentence?
3 Was the offense a misdemeanor, felony, or marijuana-related offense?
4 Which court handled it: District Court, Justice Court, City Court, Municipal Court, or another court?
5 What county?
6 What was the charge and statute number?
7 What was the conviction date?
8 When were all sentence terms completed?
9 Were all fines, fees, restitution, jail time, treatment, and probation completed?
10 Has the user had any new convictions in Montana, another state, or federal court in the five years after completing the sentence?
11 Does the user have any pending charges, open cases, or current detention?
12 Has the user ever successfully petitioned for Montana misdemeanor expungement before?
13 Was the offense assault, partner/family member assault, stalking, sexual assault, protective-order violation, DUI, or DUI-enhanced?
14 Was there a victim?
15 Was the case marijuana-related?
16 Was the person currently serving the sentence or has it been completed?
17 Was the sentence deferred and, if so, has dismissal already been entered?
18 Does the user need expungement for employment, housing, licensing, school, immigration, military enlistment, or firearms?

sourceSections.title: Wilma Decision Tree
sourceSections.text:
If Tribal, federal, or out-of-state
Route:

Montanas misdemeanor expungement packet does not cover this case. This needs jurisdiction-specific review.

If no conviction
Route:

Possible Montana DOJ CRISS non-conviction removal or sealing. Check whether the charge was dismissed, acquitted, dropped, not filed, or dismissed after deferred prosecution.

If deferred sentence not dismissed
Route:

Contact the sentencing court about dismissal after deferred imposition before record-sealing analysis.

If deferred sentence already dismissed
Route:

Records may already be confidential criminal justice information. Check whether the public record still appears and whether DOJ CRISS needs documentation.

If marijuana-related
Route:

Use Montana MMRTA pathway: Form A if currently serving sentence, Form B if completed sentence. File in the court of conviction and serve county attorney.

If misdemeanor conviction
Route:

Check five-year completion rule, no pending charges, no new conviction, sentence completion, and prior expungement history.

If misdemeanor is in not-presumed category
Route:

Expungement is not presumed. Collect rehabilitation evidence and recommend attorney or legal clinic review.

If felony, non-marijuana
Route:

No general Montana felony expungement path through the misdemeanor packet. Check for deferred-sentence dismissal, marijuana-specific relief, pardon, or attorney review.

sourceSections.title: Mandatory Escalation Flags
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should recommend attorney or legal-clinic review if any of these apply:
Felony conviction.
Marijuana felony or resentencing/redesignation issue.
Tribal court, federal court, or out-of-state conviction.
Deferred sentence confusion.
Pending charge or open case.
New conviction during the five-year period.
Prior use of Montana misdemeanor expungement.
Assault, partner/family member assault, stalking, sexual assault, protective-order violation, DUI, or DUI-enhanced offense.
Victim objection risk.
Immigration consequences.
Firearms rights issue.
Professional licensing issue.
Military enlistment or military-position issue.
Incomplete criminal history.
Multiple courts/counties.
User wants a guaranteed outcome.

15. Wilma
Do Not Say
Rules
Wilma should not say:

Your record will definitely be erased.

Nobody can ever see it again.

You qualify for sure.

A felony can be expunged in Montana
without checking marijuana or deferred-sentence context.

DUI is impossible to expunge.

This clears your FBI record.

This fixes immigration.

This restores gun rights.

You can file again later if you forget a misdemeanor.

Private background check companies will automatically update.

Wilma may say:

Based on what you shared, you may have a Montana record-clearing pathway. The next step is to verify the official court record, Montana DOJ criminal history, sentence completion date, pending-case status, and whether the case is a misdemeanor, non-conviction, deferred sentence, or marijuana-related conviction.

sourceSections.title: Montana Filing Packet Checklist
sourceSections.text:
Misdemeanor conviction packet
Collect:
Full legal name.
Current address, phone, email.
Court where filing.
Every misdemeanor conviction to include.
County and court for each conviction.
Case numbers, if known.
Charge names and statute numbers.
Conviction dates.
Final judgments.
Proof sentence completed.
Proof fines/fees/restitution paid.
Proof probation/treatment/jail complete.
Montana DOJ CRISS record.
Public court portal search results.
Prosecutor offices for service.
Victim issue, if any.
Not-presumed offense issue, if any.
Fee waiver form, if needed.
Proposed order.
Certificate of service.
Marijuana-related packet
Collect:
Montana DOJ criminal record.
Court of conviction.
Conviction date or approximate date.
Charge/statute.
Whether sentence is current or completed.
Whether requesting expungement, resentencing, or redesignation.
Form A or Form B.
Certificate of service on county attorney.
Proposed order.
Filing fee or fee waiver.
DOJ Expungement/Removal Request after order.
Non-conviction removal
Collect:
Montana DOJ CRISS record.
Court disposition showing dismissal, acquittal, dropped charge, not filed, deferred prosecution dismissed, or invalidated conviction.
CRISS non-conviction removal request.
Any missing court documentation needed by CRISS.

sourceSections.title: Recommended Wilma Output Format
sourceSections.text:
Montana Record-Clearing Screening Summary

1. Case Type:
[Misdemeanor conviction / non-conviction / deferred sentence / marijuana-related / felony / Tribal / federal / out-of-state]

sourceSections.title: Waiting Period:
sourceSections.text: [Five years met / not met / not applicable / unknown]

sourceSections.title: Prior Expungement:
sourceSections.text:
[No prior use / prior use / unknown]

10. Risk Flags:
[Felony, DUI, assault, PFMA, stalking, sexual assault, protective order, victim, immigration, firearms, licensing, Tribal, federal, out-of-state]

11. Next Step:
[Get CRISS record, get final judgment, complete misdemeanor packet, complete MMRTA Form A/B, request deferred dismissal, or consult attorney]

12. Disclaimer:

sourceSections.title: Montana Quick Reference
sourceSections.text:
Topic Montana Rule
Main misdemeanor law MCA Title 46, Chapter 18, Part 11
Main misdemeanor petition statute 46-18-1104
Venue District Court where at least one misdemeanor conviction occurred
Waiting period Generally 5 years after completing sentence terms
Pending charges No current detention, charges, or pending new offense
Once in lifetime Yes
Fingerprints Required for identity validation
Not-presumed offenses Assault, PFMA, stalking, sexual assault, protective order, DUI, DUI-enhanced
Court standard Preponderance of the evidence
Prosecutor notice Serve every prosecution office involved
Victim notice Prosecutor attempts notice within 14 days
Court can grant All, some, or none of the requested misdemeanors
DOJ follow-up Send order, fingerprint card, and DOJ form to CRISS
Non-convictions CRISS removal/sealing under 44-5-202 process
Deferred sentence Dismissal can make records confidential
Marijuana Separate MMRTA forms and process
Felony, non-marijuana Generally not covered by misdemeanor expungement
Tribal/out-of-state/federal Not covered by Montana misdemeanor packet
This Montana reference is ready for Wilma training, intake triage, eligibility screening, document routing, and escalation logic.

frontendMustNot: select legal pathways without the backend result
render a paid packet action for an automatic, guidance-only, waiting, missing-info, review, ineligible, or hard-stop result

qa.reviewStatus: source_compiled; attorney/source-freshness/form-visual review still required before production
